title: The Climate Hazards Center Infrared Precipitation with Stations, Version 3
why_relevant: Primary CHIRPS v3 data citation; documents station/satellite/climatology construction and v3 improvements.
project_decision: Use as the primary precipitation-data citation and keep PRISM validation as an independent check.

title: Spatiotemporal characterization of drought in Botswana: a regionalization study using long-term CHIRPS satellite data
why_relevant: Closest methodological analogue for CHIRPS SPI-12 regionalization, homogeneous drought zones, run theory, and teleconnection diagnostics.
project_decision: Use regionalization as mechanism evidence only; do not claim it proves forecast skill.

title: Probabilistic Seasonal Forecasts in the North American Multimodel Ensemble: A Baseline Skill Assessment
why_relevant: Establishes BSS, reliability, and resolution as proper metrics for NMME probabilistic tercile forecasts.
project_decision: Supports our NMME probability benchmark and Murphy-style reliability framing.

title: Seasonal Drought Forecasting for Latin America Using the ECMWF S4 Forecast System
why_relevant: Shows SPI forecast skill depends strongly on season, region, and accumulation period, and is tested against climatology.
project_decision: Use to frame seasonal SPI-3/SPI-6 results as expected to be conditional, not uniformly positive.

title: Catchment memory explains hydrological drought forecast performance
why_relevant: Shows drought forecast skill improves when the target has hydrological memory; meteorological SPI-1 is a harder low-memory target.
project_decision: Strengthens the claim that weak SPI-1 skill is physically plausible and suggests hydrological/soil-moisture targets as a stronger future direction.

title: Evaluation of Subseasonal Drought Forecast Skill over the Coastal Western United States
why_relevant: SubX-driven Noah-MP drought onset/termination forecasts over western U.S.; useful skill mostly weeks 1-2, limited by week 3, essentially none by week 4 for many events.
project_decision: Directly supports a predictability-barrier interpretation for month-ahead drought events in western U.S. settings.

title: Subseasonal Forecast Skill of Evaporative Demand, Soil Moisture, and Flash Drought Onset from Two Dynamic Models over the Contiguous United States
why_relevant: Evaluates GEOS/GEFSv12 SubX ETo, RZSM, and flash-drought indices; predictability declines by weeks 3-4 and depends on target variable.
project_decision: Use as support for dynamic-model and land-surface-state benchmarks rather than another lag-only ML model.

title: Skillful subseasonal soil moisture drought forecasts with deep learning-dynamic models
why_relevant: Recent strong positive example: skillful root-zone soil-moisture drought forecasts use antecedent RZSM plus dynamic-model forecasts, not precipitation lags alone.
project_decision: If seeking a stronger positive model, pivot to forecast-informed RZSM/soil-moisture targets; do not expect EDL/ConvLSTM on CHIRPS-only SPI-1 to solve the problem.

title: Improving Subseasonal Forecasting in the Western U.S. with Machine Learning
why_relevant: ML subseasonal forecast study using many meteorological predictors plus dynamic forecasts and explicit benchmarks.
project_decision: Supports adding operational forecast predictors when the goal is positive forecast skill.

title: SubseasonalClimateUSA: A Dataset for Subseasonal Forecasting and Benchmarking
why_relevant: Benchmark dataset and toolkit for subseasonal U.S. prediction with climatology, persistence, operational dynamical models, and ML/deep learning.
project_decision: Useful template for benchmark-first framing and for a future dynamic-forecast-input extension.

title: DroughtCast: A Machine Learning Forecast of the United States Drought Monitor
why_relevant: USDM forecasting with RNNs, precipitation, soil moisture, temperature, and other remote-sensing drivers; discusses spatial and temporal holdouts.
project_decision: Supports our concern about temporal generalization and the importance of soil moisture/temperature variables for operational drought targets.

title: DroughtSet: Understanding Drought Through Spatial-Temporal Learning
why_relevant: Recent drought ML benchmark with spatiotemporal learning, multiple drought indices, static and dynamic predictors, and interpretability.
project_decision: Useful as evidence that stronger ML drought claims usually require richer multi-driver datasets and benchmark datasets.

title: Explainable artificial intelligence to explore the intrinsic characteristics of climatic parameters governing meteorological drought forecasting
why_relevant: Uses SHAP/LIME for SPI/SPEI drought forecasting and finds lagged drought-index terms dominate many scenarios.
project_decision: Use SHAP as mechanism/audit evidence; require stability/ablation agreement before making driver claims.

title: Evidential Deep Learning: Enhancing Predictive Uncertainty Estimation for Earth System Science Applications
why_relevant: Shows EDL can be useful in Earth-system settings when uncertainty is evaluated by calibration and error-uncertainty association.
project_decision: EDL should be treated as a UQ method only if we evaluate calibration, sharpness, and error association against baselines.

title: Are Uncertainty Quantification Capabilities of Evidential Deep Learning a Mirage?
why_relevant: Cautions that EDL uncertainty may be unreliable without careful validation and can behave more like OOD scoring than faithful epistemic uncertainty.
project_decision: Do not make EDL a central contribution unless we add rigorous UQ diagnostics and compare against ensemble/dropout/calibration baselines.

title: A review on machine learning models for drought monitoring and forecasting
why_relevant: Recent open-access review emphasizing input variables, lead time, interpretability, regional transferability, and operationalization as persistent gaps.
project_decision: Supports framing our work as a rigorous benchmark/evaluation audit rather than a model novelty paper.

title: Subseasonal Great Plains Rainfall via Remote Extratropical Teleconnections: Regional Application of Theory-Guided Causal Networks
why_relevant: Supports the idea that Great Plains hydroclimate has subseasonal teleconnection windows, but those signals are regional and event-dependent.
project_decision: Use to motivate regional mechanism analysis in Southern Great Plains, not to claim universal forecast skill.

title: A machine learning model for drought tracking and forecasting using remote precipitation data and a standardized precipitation index from arid regions
why_relevant: CHIRPS/SPI drought-forecasting example with annual SPI-12 prediction and high reported R2; useful contrast because it targets a smoother memory-bearing SPI scale.
project_decision: Use to explain why positive CHIRPS/SPI results do not directly contradict the stricter monthly SPI-1 probability task.

title: Prediction of meteorological drought and standardized precipitation index based on RF, RT, and GPR models
why_relevant: Representative deterministic SPI-prediction ML study using RF/RT/GPR; useful for protocol-comparability audit.
project_decision: Use only as a contrast in target/metric/split design unless full methods are audited.

title: Short lead time standard precipitation index forecasting: Extreme learning machine and variational mode decomposition
why_relevant: Short-lead SPI forecasting with signal decomposition and ELM; illustrates another successful but different SPI-forecast family.
project_decision: Use to distinguish target-series/decomposition forecasting from leakage-safe dry-fraction probability forecasting.

title: Hyperparameter tuning and performance assessment of statistical and machine-learning algorithms using spatial data
why_relevant: Methodological evidence that non-spatial/random resampling can overestimate performance with spatially autocorrelated data.
project_decision: Use as support for spatial pseudo-replication guardrails without accusing individual drought papers of leakage.
